author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

author: unnas
content: I used to enter into these kind of trades when I was first learning about the break and retest strategy.  Every time I saw a good retest, I would take the trade (good thing it was only paper money back then).  The biggest lesson I learned is to develop a HTF thesis first, then enter a set up that lines up with the HTF thesis.  For example, we are currently in a downtrend and most stocks (including NVDA) are in a downtrend.  Therefore, a suggested setup would be to look for a shorting position on a key level.  I make sure to watch Jdubs premarket prep videos to get an idea of what to expect and what key levels to keep and eye on.  Trying to a Long position on a stock that is downtrending in the HTF is pretty risky.  Also, something new that I'm just learning is to use the QQQ and SPY price action to determine relative strength and weakness for the stock I'm considering to trade.  In a nut shell, the break and retest strategy works best when you pair it up with multiple confluences like HTF thesis, current market direction, and relative strength/weakness.  I'm very new to trading, but what' I'm learning so far is that simply applying the break and retest to a key level isn't optimal.  We need to start line up the strategy with multiple confluences.

author: Tito Frescado
content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

author: Kam
content:
Took a pretty lousy trade on PLTR this morning and wanted to share my review. Open to any thoughts or other feedback! 

PLTR – 6/26/25
Setup: BnR 1m OB Continuation + 5m OR Retest
Result:  Loss (–1R)
Confidence on Entry: 3/5
Sizing: Base Size

What Happened:
Strong opening drive in PLTR with RS and news catalyst. Took an anticipatory reclaim entry off the 1m OB after visual strength near prior pivot, but skipped confirmation and entered on candle stretch. Market context was improving, 5m candle looked like a nice hammer, but pullback was steep and PLTR can be trappy. Trade hit stop shortly after entry. Earlier missed trade off PMH may have been a factor as well as pass on NVDA OB play prior day.

Classification:
False Form – Anticipatory Entry 
Good structure idea, poor entry execution. Trade plan shifted from OB pullback to reclaim without re-evaluation.

Primary Mistake:
Premature Entry – No Reclaim Confirmation

What Worked:
- Avoided forced dip buys in semis
- Honored stop and respected system
- Solid structure read on RS and catalyst

Needs Work:
– Entry discipline on reclaims
– Slight emotional residue from passing on NVDA prior day and PMH retest at the open
– OB sequence clarity and reclaim filters need reinforcement

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

author: 305 Trader
content:
hi Francine. I thought of making a review to your trade, because I found it pretty good a teaching for me. You got the direction right, as you can see Apple had been on a upward ride for days. So, yes momentum had been there, thats 1. Now as for your entry, since AAPL demonstrated that the uptrend was clear for several days, that bounce from your wedge in purple lines was your POI and not the ORB you drew. Additionally, the break out from that wedge had confluence with one of your KL. This was a powerful event you should have not missed.  If I was to take a swing or even a day trade on AAPL, it would have been on the break and retest (in a smaller tf) of that confluence. In the higher timeframe (swing) this was also, the continuation signal of the market in its uptrend. So, your entry was late when you decided to make it off of that ORB way above. 
Or, if taking the swing off of that ORB, it would need to be after the mitigation with a strong rejection to that POI or ORB you drew. I think you took it on the 1st impulse instead  and hence you had the expected pullback. I am curious how the symbol did on Friday since I had not traded since last Tuesday.

author: EeveeWinter
content: You were in a 332.5C, with the underlying moving to 335.10. But for most of the trade: You were initially OTM — The move took time — Delta was likely sub-0.50 for a good chunk of the time. Since delta starts low and only ramps up as price nears the strike, your option didn’t gain value linearly. Early in the move, delta was low; as the move slowed, delta rose — but then theta canceled the gain.

author: Royal191
content: Hi Anne, glad to see you are able to identify the issue so quickly. Now you can make a plan/rules to avoid this happening in the future. Revenge trading is one of those things that will wreck your mental capital and your actual capital. Especially revenge trading with names like TSLA or META. If you take a bad trade, try to step away from the screens for a few mins and clear your head. If you decide to come back and look for another trade you have to treat the next trade as its own trade and trade it without the emotions from the prev trades. Once you have the prev loss in your mind it will be easier to make mistakes like taking profits too early or even holding too long to make up the loss and letting a green trade turn red.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Hi Max. Thanks for sharing your trades which lost, I think is brave of you to decide to do self accountabilty on your losses and to share. We learn a lot from our losses and other people’s.,I took the time to draw some thoughts and took screenshots of your charts. Then I draw where I would enter, not knowing anything about HTF and a narrative on those tickers, just purely intraday patterns from the chart.  I trade patterns. 

I know it may be a different style for you. I think the market always form patterns like a gradual ascending (long) staircase, or a gradual descending (short) staircase. These patterns repeat over and over intraday but it needs time to form. 

Trading the 1st 15 mins of market open in my opinion requires a very strong HTF narrative formed, a strong understanding of the stock and a good gut for being ok with accepting larger losses.  And this is because the market has not formed patterns yet, its only reaction to pre market and gaps. 
I trade differently. This is what I watch for, I follow a staircase formation which breaks price structure along  key levels of confluence and only act when a key level is respected as support or resistance to go in continuation. I think Neto trades this way more often than the other coaches. Its a matter of choice and backtesting. Very important this b/t part. 

Lastly, one advice, do not do initial mental stop loss. This is only for traders who have an extraordinary emotional management achieved and a rigurous discipline aka Jack or Tony.  I am not exaggerating. Our job is to defend our profits. The market’s is to provide the wins. 

Here is my screenshots of where I would have liked to enter safely.

author: jav
content: Hey Max, I am certainly not someone to be giving advice but somethings I have learned and advice I have been given that you may find useful. In regards to a mental stop or a hard stop, with a hard stop if the candle hits your stop area your going to be stopped out of the trade, which is why Tony and Jack always say to wait for candle closure. I used to always use a hard stop but I would mostly get stopped out and then the stock would take off in the way I wanted it to, which can be very nerve racking. I am not sure what your set up was on those 2 trades but Tony advises you to stay with one set up when you are a new trader, whether that be the 5 minute opening range, premarket or previous day and just stay with whichever your most comfortable with. I personally only trade the 5 minute opening range break. I also switched to a 5 minute chart because trading off a 1 minute chart I found to be super stressful. The only con to a 5 minute chart when waiting for candle closure is if you get a big candle the opposite of your trade your stop is going to be much lower than you wanted it to be, but that has only happened once in TSLA.  I hope you found something useful in this and good luck in your trading journey.

author: 305 Trader
content: Setting up the correct stop loss (where the trade invalidates) at start is a real art guys and sticking to it with a mechanical stop loss is the only way to create a good habit of accepting the risk of trading. No hope, just a mechanical trade plan implemented with discipline. Mental stop losses do not help us create good habits. They are helpful when the disciplined trader has become the operator. Before this, mental stop losses are a demon for me.

No worries, glad it helped. Love that attitude, keep it up! 

Definitely backtest the 5m closures so that you can find what works for your style/system. 

Waiting for 5m closes can widen the stops sometimes and kill the R/R so stop placement is pretty critical and keep time of day top of mind. 

For stops, focus on the level that's being reclaimed (if trading a reclaim). I used to be really bad  at reclaims when I first joined this room (ask <@1062934985109160057> 😂), but now they're becoming one of my best setups so definitely keep practicing! 

Generally from my data it's ok for a level to be retested after a reclaim, but these setups should move pretty quickly so don't want to give too much room.

I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:

- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!

- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades

content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Running hard stops is completely fine just always be sure to be aware of what the other time frames are shaping up like compared to the one you are trading on, and have a proper and defined risk. Jack and Tony emphasize the importance of a candle close so eventually you will get the grasp after enough wick outs then price running straight to TP, you will be able to identify how to avoid it in the future. I have saved my myself from fake invalidations and had very good trades play out by not letting a wick stop me, but I’ve also had some trades seriously run away by waiting for candle close. So It has its pros and cons and eventually, based on your own trading style, you will find out what works best for you and a lot of times it is based on situational context. But in the meantime I do strongly recommend a proper and defined risk level if you are running hard stops. I used to trade the 1M as well, but now I only enter and scale on it. My risk and target is always managed and defined on the 5 and 15 and its taken a lot of the unnecessary losses out, stopped me from entering fakeout/sweeps, and improved my win rate and trading overall.

author: DWalt Trades
content: I recommend doing trade review videos on all your trades, bar by bar in replay mode, considering more than one time frame, everyday after session. Along with mass backtesting sessions that are recorded. This will help you identify how and where to cut down the overtrading and hold your trades until they either are invalidated or objective filled. I say this because technically your first entry @ 25,798.50 was never invalidated, and your initial trade could’ve been held to main objectives instead of having multiple smaller trades inside of the initial main position. This could have been a one and done day. I used to find myself in trade reviews saying “I should have just held my initial entry” on a breakeven or small red day that could’ve very well been green days and ultimately those deep reviews are where my improvement came from. That is the main reason I responded to your first text in here about it because I immediately identified something that I used to fall victim to as well on your chart so any questions just tag me here and I am happy to help!
